commentText: This one really sounds like us. The Robert story is perfect — specific, emotional, and not overwrought. The P.S. about 94% going to programs is a great trust signal. This is the closest to ready-to-publish.
Feels generic and corporate. 'We hope this message finds you well' is a red flag. No story, no personality. I don't think our donors would feel moved by this.
Good effort — the Maria story works well. The ask is clear. Loses some warmth in the middle paragraph. The closing feels slightly rushed but it's usable with light edits.
The bullet list format doesn't feel right for a fundraising email — too transactional. The tone is okay but the structure makes it feel like a product page, not a donor letter.
Best of the four by far. The quote opening is exactly how we'd write this ourselves. I'd use this with minimal changes.
Overall: Claude is clearly the strongest. GPT-5.5 is a solid second. Internal tool needs to stop using bullet lists in donor emails. Gemini needs more personality.
The Maria story is relatable but the ask feels buried. Would move the CTA earlier. Solid writing — second best of this batch.
Concise, but in a clinical way. Hits the required info without any warmth. If I were a donor I wouldn't feel invited into the mission here.
The opening line is fine but it falls apart in the middle. Too many facts stacked without a narrative spine. Would need a significant rewrite before sending.
Big improvement over Run 1. The impact number lede is compelling. The P.S. reinforces the impact well. This is now competitive with Claude.
Still the best. The waiting list framing is urgent without being guilt-inducing. Exactly the right tone.
Still feels like a form letter. Even with the impact number, the voice is flat. Not sure Gemini is the right fit for this type of content.
The revision shows. Impact number up front is a smart change, and the children's section story works well. I'd edit the last paragraph but the bones are good.
Consistently strong. The waiting list angle creates urgency without being alarmist. Feels genuine and donor-first.
Still missing a human story. Reads like an annual report excerpt. Even adding one specific person or moment would help considerably.
Better than run 1 but still too structured for donor email. The bullet points might work internally but not for a fundraising appeal.
Passable first draft. Formatting is a little stiff but the core message is there. Can see the tool improving with better prompting.
This is a strong step forward. The Denise example is concrete, the ask is clean, and the donor feels connected without being pressured. Very usable.
Best of the run again. The library card detail feels specific and true to Acme. It is concise, warm, and clear about what $50 funds.
Improved from Run 2, but still more informational than moving. It technically follows the prompt but the voice does not feel donor-centered enough.
Overall: GPT-5.5 and Claude are now both publishable with light edits. Internal is serviceable. Gemini is safer but still too flat for fundraising.
Strong improvement. The Denise example grounds the email in something real and the handoff to the ask feels natural rather than forced.
The library card framing is clever — specific without being sentimental. This earns a 'ready to send' with almost no edits.
Getting more polished but still cautious. Each sentence is technically fine; there's just no voice stitching them together. Feels assembled rather than written.
Better than before — no bullet lists this time, which is progress. The middle paragraph is flat but the tone is closer to where we need it.
Structure finally feels like an actual letter. Needs a stronger opener but I could work with this.
Excellent opening. The medication instruction example is exactly the kind of daily-life detail we wanted. The P.S. is a little poetic but still on brand.
This is probably the cleanest version so far. Plain language, specific example, no guilt. I would use this as the base draft.
The internal tool has improved a lot now that the prompt discourages abstractions. Still slightly plain, but the structure finally feels like an email rather than notes.
Very strong. The medication instruction detail is exactly the right kind of specific. Neck and neck with Claude this round.
Clean and direct. The specific example lands well and the donor never feels guilted. A few minor word choice tweaks and this is ready to go.
Noticeably better voice this round. Still more informational than emotionally resonant, but I'd call it acceptable for a conservative send.
Language is cleaner than previous runs. Not as warm as GPT-5.5 or Claude but could work for donors who prefer a more professional tone.
The internal tool has turned a corner. It's not going to win any awards but it no longer feels like it was generated by a spreadsheet.
Strongest final candidate. Rosa's detail is vivid without feeling invented for drama, and the donor partnership language lands well.
Very close to Claude. The work schedule example is practical and the ask is direct. I might trim one sentence, but otherwise this is ready.
Still sounds like a template. Accurate and low risk, but it does not carry enough emotional specificity for donor email copy.
Overall: by Run 5, Claude and GPT-5.5 are effectively tied for usable first drafts. Recommend retiring Gemini from this eval unless we need a conservative baseline.
Rosa's story works. Clear purpose, emotionally honest without manipulation, and the partnership language doesn't feel like a cliché this time. My top pick this run.
Excellent. The work schedule framing gives donors something concrete to imagine, which is exactly right. Would use this almost verbatim.
Solid structure, no obvious errors, but it still reads like it was produced by committee. The individual voice just isn't there.
Major improvement. The story is simpler than Claude and GPT-5.5 but it's coherent and honest. Would send this if the others weren't available.
Came a long way from run 1. The voice is warmer and the ask is clean. Still third behind Claude and GPT-5.5, but genuinely usable now.
The library magic line is a standout. 'You never know who in your network has been quietly waiting for this' — that's share-worthy copy. Would use the Facebook and Instagram versions almost as-is.
Too listy and announcement-style. The bullet point format works for some content but social media needs more personality than this.
